findings.dominantFinding: The dominant imaging finding is a cortically based, expansile intra-axial mass that demonstrates contrast enhancement. The lesion causes local cortical expansion and exhibits variable signal characteristics depending on its specific grade, typically appearing iso- to hypointense on T1-weighted images and hyperintense on T2-weighted images.
findings.supportingFeatures: Supporting features include the presence of adjacent vasogenic edema and potential cystic areas or mural nodules, particularly if this represents a pleomorphic xanthoastrocytoma (PXA). There may also be associated remodeling of the overlying inner table of the calvarium due to its slow-growing, peripheral nature.
findings.criticalNegatives: Importantly, there is no evidence of a 'bubbly' or wedge-shaped pseudocystic appearance pointing towards a dysembryoplastic neuroepithelial tumor (DNET). Furthermore, there is no dense, clumped calcification characteristic of an oligodendroglioma.
findings.influentialFeature: The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of an enhancing, cortically based intra-axial mass without the classical coarse calcifications of an oligodendroglioma or the characteristic wedge-shape of a DNET.

differentials.primaryDiagnosis: The appearances are most in keeping with Astrocytoma, given the cortically based enhancing mass that lacks defining features of other specific cortical tumors. Certain subtypes of astrocytomas, such as pleomorphic xanthoastrocytomas, characteristically present as peripheral, cortically based cystic lesions with an enhancing mural nodule.
differentials.principleDifferential: The principle differential would be Oligodendroglioma, however this classically presents with prominent coarse calcifications that 'bloom' on gradient echo sequences, which are absent here. Additionally, oligodendrogliomas often demonstrate a more slowly progressive, infiltrative pattern with minimal vasogenic edema.
differentials.excludeDiagnosis: A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Ganglioglioma, given it is the most common cause of temporal lobe epilepsy in young patients and misdiagnosing it could alter the surgical approach for seizure control. However, gangliogliomas classically present as a mixed cystic and solid mass with a strongly enhancing mural nodule and frequent calcification, which may not perfectly match these solid enhancing appearances.

management.priority: From a radiological perspective, the priority is to characterise the lesion's margins, assess for restricted diffusion to grade the tumor, and plan for potential surgical resection.
management.priorImaging: I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is crucial to determine the growth rate of the lesion and to evaluate for any signs of malignant transformation, such as new enhancement, increasing edema, or necrosis.
management.furtherImaging: I would recommend a dedicated MRI of the brain with contrast if only a CT has been performed, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely delineating cortical involvement, detecting subtle cystic components, and mapping adjacent eloquent white matter tracts.
management.mriSequences: I would specify high-resolution T2 and FLAIR sequences to delineate the extent of cortical expansion and surrounding edema, alongside susceptibility-weighted imaging to exclude subtle calcification, and pre- and post-contrast T1 sequences to assess the enhancement pattern.
management.intervention: If a definitive tissue diagnosis is required to differentiate from other cortical neoplasms, I would specify a stereotactic image-guided biopsy targeting the solid, enhancing component.
management.mdtDiscussion: This should go to the Neuro-oncology MDT. My report would clearly document the exact lobar location of the mass, involvement of the overlying cortex, and relationship to eloquent areas to guide safe surgical resection.

vivaAnswer.fullScript:
The dominant imaging finding is a cortically based, expansile intra-axial mass that demonstrates contrast enhancement. The lesion causes local cortical expansion and exhibits variable signal characteristics depending on its specific grade, typically appearing iso- to hypointense on T1-weighted images and hyperintense on T2-weighted images. Supporting features include the presence of adjacent vasogenic edema and potential cystic areas or mural nodules, particularly if this represents a pleomorphic xanthoastrocytoma (PXA). There may also be associated remodeling of the overlying inner table of the calvarium due to its slow-growing, peripheral nature. Importantly, there is no evidence of a 'bubbly' or wedge-shaped pseudocystic appearance pointing towards a dysembryoplastic neuroepithelial tumor (DNET). Furthermore, there is no dense, clumped calcification characteristic of an oligodendroglioma. The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of an enhancing, cortically based intra-axial mass without the classical coarse calcifications of an oligodendroglioma or the characteristic wedge-shape of a DNET.

The appearances are most in keeping with Astrocytoma, given the cortically based enhancing mass that lacks defining features of other specific cortical tumors. Certain subtypes of astrocytomas, such as pleomorphic xanthoastrocytomas, characteristically present as peripheral, cortically based cystic lesions with an enhancing mural nodule. The principle differential would be Oligodendroglioma, however this classically presents with prominent coarse calcifications that 'bloom' on gradient echo sequences, which are absent here. Additionally, oligodendrogliomas often demonstrate a more slowly progressive, infiltrative pattern with minimal vasogenic edema. A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Ganglioglioma, given it is the most common cause of temporal lobe epilepsy in young patients and misdiagnosing it could alter the surgical approach for seizure control. However, gangliogliomas classically present as a mixed cystic and solid mass with a strongly enhancing mural nodule and frequent calcification, which may not perfectly match these solid enhancing appearances.

From a radiological perspective, the priority is to characterise the lesion's margins, assess for restricted diffusion to grade the tumor, and plan for potential surgical resection. I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is crucial to determine the growth rate of the lesion and to evaluate for any signs of malignant transformation, such as new enhancement, increasing edema, or necrosis. I would recommend a dedicated MRI of the brain with contrast if only a CT has been performed, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely delineating cortical involvement, detecting subtle cystic components, and mapping adjacent eloquent white matter tracts. I would specify high-resolution T2 and FLAIR sequences to delineate the extent of cortical expansion and surrounding edema, alongside susceptibility-weighted imaging to exclude subtle calcification, and pre- and post-contrast T1 sequences to assess the enhancement pattern. If a definitive tissue diagnosis is required to differentiate from other cortical neoplasms, I would specify a stereotactic image-guided biopsy targeting the solid, enhancing component. This should go to the Neuro-oncology MDT. My report would clearly document the exact lobar location of the mass, involvement of the overlying cortex, and relationship to eloquent areas to guide safe surgical resection.
